title: The eye — how we see
examTip: The eye gets tested in two main ways: name a structure and state its function, or explain accommodation and vision defects. Both are very learnable. The accommodation mechanism — which muscles do what for near and far objects — is the one students most often get backwards. Get that right and you'll stand out.
introTips: You're going to learn how light gets turned into a nerve signal, and how the eye adjusts to see things at different distances — a process called accommodation.
AQA loves to test the functions of specific eye structures. There are about six to know; link each to its function as you learn it.
Vision defects (short-sightedness and long-sightedness) follow from understanding where the image forms — once you understand that, the correction makes logical sense.
intro: You're reading this right now because light bounced off a screen, passed through your cornea and lens, and landed precisely on your retina — and your eye made tiny adjustments to focus it without you doing anything. The eye is a remarkably precise optical instrument, and when something in that process goes slightly wrong (as it does for millions of people), the result is blurred vision. Understanding the mechanisms — how it focuses, how it adjusts to light levels, and what goes wrong in short and long-sightedness — is exactly what AQA tests in this section.

keyPoints.heading: Eye structures and their functions
keyPoints.content: The eye has several key structures, each with a specific role. The cornea is the transparent outer layer that does most of the bending (refraction) of light — it provides about 70% of the eye's focusing power. The iris is the coloured ring of muscle that controls the size of the pupil (the hole in the centre) — it adjusts how much light enters. The lens fine-tunes focus by changing shape. The retina is the light-sensitive layer at the back of the eye, containing two types of receptor cells: rods (sensitive to low light levels; black and white vision) and cones (sensitive to colour; require bright light). The optic nerve carries electrical impulses from the retina to the brain.
writeBullets: Cornea: refracts (bends) light — provides most of the eye's focusing power
Iris: controls pupil size — regulates how much light enters the eye
Pupil: the hole in the iris — larger in dim light, smaller in bright light
Lens: fine-tunes focus by changing shape (accommodation)
Retina: contains rods (low light, monochrome) and cones (colour, bright light)
Optic nerve: carries impulses from retina to the brain

keyTerms.def: Transparent outer layer of the eye; refracts light and provides most of the eye's focusing power
The coloured ring of muscle that controls pupil size and therefore how much light enters the eye
The hole in the centre of the iris through which light passes
The flexible structure behind the iris that fine-tunes focus by changing shape
The light-sensitive layer at the back of the eye containing rod and cone receptor cells
Receptor cells in the retina sensitive to low light levels; detect monochrome (black and white)
Receptor cells in the retina sensitive to colour; require bright light
The nerve that carries electrical impulses from the retina to the brain

keyPoints.memTip: For the six main structures, build a table: structure → what it does → one key fact. Do it without looking. The exam will give you a diagram and ask you to label — knowing the function of each part is what turns a label into a mark.

keyPoints.heading: Accommodation — focusing near and far
keyPoints.content: Accommodation is the process by which the eye changes the shape of the lens to focus on objects at different distances. The lens is connected to the ciliary muscles by the suspensory ligaments. For a near object: the ciliary muscles contract → suspensory ligaments go slack → lens becomes fatter/rounder → light is refracted more → image focused on retina. For a distant object: the ciliary muscles relax → suspensory ligaments are pulled taut → lens becomes thinner/flatter → light is refracted less → image focused on retina. This is the relationship students most often reverse — learn it carefully.
writeBullets: Accommodation = changing lens shape to focus at different distances
Near object: ciliary muscles CONTRACT → ligaments slack → lens fat/round → more refraction
Far object: ciliary muscles RELAX → ligaments taut → lens thin/flat → less refraction
Fatter lens = more refraction = image of near object brought into focus
Thinner lens = less refraction = image of distant object brought into focus
Suspensory ligaments connect the ciliary muscles to the lens

keyTerms.def: The process of changing lens shape to focus on objects at different distances
Ring of muscle around the lens; contracts or relaxes to change lens shape
Fibres connecting the ciliary muscles to the lens; transmit tension to flatten or relax the lens

keyPoints.memTip: Near = muscles contract = lens fat. Far = muscles relax = lens flat. 'Near needs more bending — fat lens bends more. Far needs less bending — flat lens bends less.' The muscle and ligament relationship is the opposite of what most people expect: muscles contracting makes the ligaments slack, not tight.

keyPoints.heading: The pupil reflex — adjusting for light
keyPoints.content: The pupil reflex controls how much light enters the eye by changing the size of the pupil. In bright light: the circular muscles of the iris contract → pupil constricts (gets smaller) → less light enters → retina protected from damage. In dim light: the radial muscles of the iris contract → pupil dilates (gets wider) → more light enters → better vision in low light. This is a reflex — it happens automatically, without conscious thought. You can observe it by watching someone's pupils when they move between dark and bright conditions.
writeBullets: Bright light → circular muscles contract → pupil constricts → less light enters
Dim light → radial muscles contract → pupil dilates → more light enters
Pupil reflex is automatic and unconscious
Pupil size controlled by the iris (not the cornea or lens)

keyTerms.def: The automatic adjustment of pupil size in response to light levels
Muscles of the iris that contract to constrict the pupil in bright light
Muscles of the iris that contract to dilate the pupil in dim light

keyPoints.memTip: Circular = Constricts. Both start with C. Radial = dilates in dim light. Picture the iris like a wheel — the radial muscles go outwards like spokes, pulling the pupil open.

keyPoints.heading: Vision defects — short-sightedness and long-sightedness
keyPoints.content: Short-sightedness (myopia): the eyeball is too long, or the lens is too curved, so the image of a distant object forms in front of the retina rather than on it. Close objects are seen clearly; distant objects are blurred. Corrected with a diverging (concave) lens, which spreads the light rays out before they enter the eye so the image forms further back, on the retina. Long-sightedness (hyperopia): the eyeball is too short, or the lens is not curved enough, so the image of a near object would form behind the retina. Close objects are blurred; distant objects may be seen clearly. Corrected with a converging (convex) lens, which brings the light rays together so the image forms on the retina. Both defects can also be corrected permanently by laser eye surgery, which reshapes the cornea.
writeBullets: Short-sightedness: image forms in FRONT of retina — distant objects blurred
Short-sightedness correction: diverging (concave) lens
Long-sightedness: image forms BEHIND retina — near objects blurred
Long-sightedness correction: converging (convex) lens
Laser eye surgery: reshapes the cornea permanently to correct the defect
Contact lenses and spectacles work the same way as the corrective lenses

keyTerms.def: A vision defect where the image of a distant object forms in front of the retina; corrected with a diverging lens
A vision defect where the image of a near object forms behind the retina; corrected with a converging lens
A lens that spreads light rays apart — used to correct short-sightedness
A lens that brings light rays together — used to correct long-sightedness
A procedure that permanently reshapes the cornea to correct vision defects

keyPoints.memTip: Short-sighted = image in FRONT (too close to lens). Use Diverging lens to push it back. Long-sighted = image BEHIND (too far). Use Converging lens to pull it forward. D for Distant and Diverge both apply to short-sightedness — that's your hook.

tables.title: Short-sightedness vs long-sightedness
rows: Eyeball too long / lens too curved
Eyeball too short / lens not curved enough

0.question: Which part of the eye does most of the refraction (bending) of light?
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — the cornea does most of the light bending (about 70% of the eye's focusing power). The lens fine-tunes the focus by changing shape.
0.wrongFeedback: It's the cornea, not the lens. The cornea provides the majority of the eye's refractive power. The lens fine-tunes focus by changing shape (accommodation), but the cornea does the heavy lifting.

1.question: When focusing on a near object, what happens to the ciliary muscles and the lens?
options: Ciliary muscles relax → suspensory ligaments taut → lens becomes thinner
Ciliary muscles contract → suspensory ligaments taut → lens becomes thinner
Ciliary muscles contract → suspensory ligaments slack → lens becomes fatter
Ciliary muscles relax → suspensory ligaments slack → lens becomes fatter
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Perfect — for near objects the ciliary muscles contract, the suspensory ligaments go slack, and the lens becomes fatter. A fatter lens refracts light more, bringing the near image into focus.
1.wrongFeedback: This is the one students most often get wrong. For near objects: ciliary muscles CONTRACT → ligaments go SLACK (not taut — contracting the ring of muscle makes it smaller, reducing tension on the ligaments) → lens becomes FATTER. Fat lens = more bending = near object focused.

3.question: A student is short-sighted. Which type of corrective lens should they use and why?
options: A converging (convex) lens, because it brings light rays together to move the image back onto the retina
A diverging (concave) lens, because it spreads light rays apart so the image forms further back, on the retina
A converging (convex) lens, because it bends light less than normal
A diverging (concave) lens, because it makes the image form in front of the retina
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly right — short-sightedness means the image forms in front of the retina. A diverging lens spreads the light rays, effectively moving the focal point back so it lands on the retina.
3.wrongFeedback: Short-sightedness = image forms in FRONT of the retina. To correct this, you need to move the image back — so you use a diverging (concave) lens, which spreads light apart. Converging lenses are used for long-sightedness (image forms behind the retina).

commonMistakes: Getting the accommodation mechanism backwards — students often say muscles relax for near objects. Remember: near = contract = slack ligaments = fat lens. The ring of ciliary muscle gets smaller when it contracts, which releases tension.
Saying the lens does all the refraction — the cornea does most of the refraction. The lens fine-tunes it. This distinction sometimes appears in exam questions.
Confusing the corrective lenses for short and long-sightedness — short-sighted = diverging lens; long-sighted = converging lens. Reversing this costs two marks.
Forgetting which vision is blurred in each defect — short-sighted people struggle with DISTANT objects; long-sighted people struggle with NEAR objects.

Mixing up circular and radial muscles in the pupil reflex — circular muscles constrict the pupil (bright light); radial muscles dilate it (dim light). Both sets contract — it's just which muscles that changes.
examTips: For accommodation questions, always state what happens to the ciliary muscles AND the suspensory ligaments AND the lens — AQA mark schemes usually have three separate marks for these three components.

For vision defect questions, say where the image forms (in front of or behind the retina) AND name the corrective lens type AND explain how the lens corrects it. Three marks = three pieces of information.
Laser eye surgery is an acceptable answer for correction alongside lens-based answers — mentioning it shows broader knowledge.
The pupil reflex is often tested in the context of the nervous system — link it back to the reflex arc if the question asks about how the iris responds to light.
revisionCardBullets: Cornea: refracts most of the light entering the eye
Iris controls pupil size — bright light = constrict, dim light = dilate
Lens: changes shape to fine-tune focus (accommodation)
Retina: rods (low light, monochrome) and cones (colour, bright light)
Optic nerve carries impulses from retina to brain
Accommodation — near: ciliary muscles contract → ligaments slack → lens fat
Accommodation — far: ciliary muscles relax → ligaments taut → lens flat
Short-sightedness: image in FRONT of retina → corrected by diverging (concave) lens
Long-sightedness: image BEHIND retina → corrected by converging (convex) lens
Laser eye surgery reshapes the cornea to permanently correct both defects
